name: Advanced Attention Mechanisms
description: Efficient attention variants including MQA, GQA, FlashAttention, and sparse attention for large language models
key_facts: Multi-Query Attention (MQA) shares a single set of key-value heads across all query heads, reducing KV cache memory
Grouped-Query Attention (GQA) is a compromise between MHA and MQA, grouping query heads to share KV heads
FlashAttention computes attention in a memory-efficient way by tiling and avoiding materialization of the full attention matrix
Sliding window attention limits each token to attend to a fixed-size local window, reducing complexity from O(n^2) to O(n)
Rotary Position Embeddings (RoPE) encode relative position information directly into the attention computation
common_misconceptions: FlashAttention changes the mathematical result of attention (it computes the exact same result, just more efficiently)
Reducing KV heads always degrades model quality (GQA achieves near-MHA quality with significantly less memory)

name: Scaling LLM Architectures
description: Mixture of Experts, model scaling strategies, and architectural innovations for larger models
key_facts: Mixture of Experts (MoE) routes each token to a subset of expert networks, enabling larger models without proportional compute increase
Chinchilla scaling laws suggest that model size and training data should be scaled proportionally for optimal compute efficiency
Pipeline parallelism splits model layers across different GPUs to train models that exceed single-GPU memory
Expert parallelism distributes MoE experts across GPUs so each GPU handles a subset of experts
NVIDIA Megatron-LM is a framework for efficient training of large transformer models with various parallelism strategies
common_misconceptions: MoE models activate all parameters for every token (only a subset of experts are activated per token, reducing compute)
Scaling model size is the only way to improve performance (data quality, training methodology, and architecture choices also matter significantly)

name: Distributed Training Strategies
description: Data parallelism, model parallelism, and hybrid strategies for training LLMs across multiple GPUs
key_facts: Data parallelism replicates the model on each GPU and splits training data across replicas, synchronizing gradients
Tensor parallelism splits individual layer computations (e.g., attention heads) across GPUs within a node
Pipeline parallelism divides model layers across GPUs and processes micro-batches in a pipeline fashion
FSDP (Fully Sharded Data Parallelism) shards model parameters, gradients, and optimizer states across GPUs
NVIDIA NeMo Framework uses a 3D parallelism strategy combining data, tensor, and pipeline parallelism
common_misconceptions: Data parallelism alone can train arbitrarily large models (models exceeding single-GPU memory require model parallelism)
More GPUs always provide linear speedup (communication overhead limits scaling efficiency, especially across nodes)

name: SFT, RLHF, and DPO
description: Supervised fine-tuning and alignment techniques including RLHF and Direct Preference Optimization
key_facts: Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) trains the model on curated instruction-response pairs to follow instructions
RLHF (Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback) uses a reward model trained on human preferences to optimize the LLM policy
DPO (Direct Preference Optimization) eliminates the separate reward model by directly optimizing on preference pairs
Constitutional AI uses a set of principles to generate self-critiques and revisions for alignment
NVIDIA NeMo Aligner provides tools for SFT, RLHF, DPO, and SteerLM alignment workflows
common_misconceptions: RLHF and DPO produce identical results (they optimize different objectives and can lead to different model behaviors)
SFT alone is sufficient for alignment (SFT teaches format but RLHF/DPO are needed to align values and preferences)

name: Training Data Curation
description: Data preparation, filtering, and quality assurance for LLM training and fine-tuning
key_facts: NVIDIA NeMo Curator provides GPU-accelerated data curation tools for filtering, deduplication, and quality scoring
Exact and fuzzy deduplication prevents models from memorizing repeated content in training data
Quality filters remove low-quality, toxic, or irrelevant content from training corpora
Data mixing ratios across domains (code, web, books, scientific papers) significantly impact model capabilities
Synthetic data generation using existing LLMs can augment training data for specialized domains
common_misconceptions: More training data is always better (low-quality or duplicated data can degrade model performance)
Data curation is a one-time process (iterative refinement based on model evaluation is typically necessary)

name: Model Quantization Techniques
description: Quantization methods for reducing model size and improving inference throughput
key_facts: Post-Training Quantization (PTQ) reduces precision of pre-trained model weights without retraining
Quantization-Aware Training (QAT) simulates low-precision arithmetic during training for better quantized accuracy
FP8 quantization on NVIDIA Hopper GPUs provides near-FP16 accuracy with 2x throughput improvement
GPTQ and AWQ are popular weight-only quantization methods that preserve activation precision
TensorRT-LLM supports INT4, INT8, FP8, and mixed-precision quantization for deployment
common_misconceptions: All quantization methods produce the same quality-speed trade-off (different methods excel for different model architectures and sizes)
INT4 quantization always makes models unusable (with proper calibration, INT4 models can retain most of their original capability)

name: LLM Serving Optimization
description: Techniques for optimizing LLM inference latency, throughput, and cost in production
key_facts: Continuous (in-flight) batching processes new requests as soon as existing requests complete, maximizing GPU utilization
PagedAttention manages KV cache memory in non-contiguous pages, reducing memory waste from fragmentation
Speculative decoding uses a smaller draft model to predict multiple tokens, verified by the larger model in one pass
Prefix caching stores KV cache entries for common prompt prefixes to avoid recomputation
Time to First Token (TTFT) and tokens-per-second are the primary latency metrics for LLM serving
common_misconceptions: Static batching is sufficient for LLM serving (static batching wastes GPU time waiting for the longest sequence to complete)
KV cache memory is negligible (for long sequences, KV cache can consume more memory than the model weights themselves)

name: Production LLM Deployment with NIM
description: Deploying and managing LLMs in production using NVIDIA NIM and container orchestration
key_facts: NVIDIA NIM containers provide pre-optimized, production-ready LLM serving with auto-tuned TensorRT-LLM backends
Kubernetes-based deployment enables auto-scaling LLM inference based on request queue depth
Health checks and readiness probes ensure traffic is only sent to fully loaded model instances
Blue-green and canary deployment strategies enable safe model version updates in production
GPU resource requests in Kubernetes ensure LLM pods are scheduled on nodes with appropriate GPU hardware
common_misconceptions: LLM deployment is just serving a REST API (production requires load balancing, monitoring, scaling, and version management)
A single NIM container configuration works for all models (configuration must be tuned for model size, GPU hardware, and workload patterns)

name: LLM Monitoring and Observability
description: Monitoring deployed LLM systems for performance, quality, and cost
key_facts: Key inference metrics include request latency (P50, P95, P99), throughput (tokens/second), and queue depth
GPU utilization, memory usage, and thermal metrics should be monitored via DCGM or nvidia-smi
Prompt and response logging enables quality monitoring and debugging of production LLM behavior
Token usage tracking is essential for cost management in production LLM deployments
Drift detection identifies when model performance degrades over time due to changing input distributions
common_misconceptions: GPU utilization alone indicates serving efficiency (high utilization with poor batching can still mean low throughput)
LLM monitoring only needs infrastructure metrics (output quality monitoring is equally critical for production systems)

name: LLM Safety and Responsible AI
description: Safety techniques, guardrails, and responsible deployment practices for LLMs
key_facts: NVIDIA NeMo Guardrails provides programmable safety rails for controlling LLM input and output
Content filtering classifiers detect and block harmful, biased, or inappropriate model outputs
Red teaming systematically probes models for vulnerabilities including jailbreaks and harmful outputs
Model cards document model capabilities, limitations, training data characteristics, and intended use cases
Hallucination detection techniques compare model outputs against retrieved facts or known ground truth
common_misconceptions: RLHF alignment makes models completely safe (aligned models can still be jailbroken or produce harmful outputs)
Safety measures only need to be applied at inference time (safety starts with training data curation and continues through deployment)
